pathway_description: Irrigation infrastructure is present but insufficient, costly, or seasonally fails.
stress_description: Farmers report insufficient water for crop production, particularly for rabi and zaid seasons or during dry spells in kharif.

aer_reference.description: Central Highlands (Malwa, Bundelkhand and Eastern Satpura), hot sub-humid ecoregion

condition.expression: (swb_total_area_ha[-1] < swb_total_area_ha[0]) OR (swb_kharif_area_ha / swb_total_area_ha < 0.5 in rabi/zaid seasons)
condition.qualitative_description: Total surface water body area is declining over the multi-year record, or SWB area in rabi/zaid is less than half of kharif area, indicating rapid seasonal drying due to siltation, poor design, or inadequate storage capacity.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In sub-humid plateau terrain like Bundelkhand and Malwa, tanks and surface water bodies are the primary irrigation source for rabi crops. A declining trend in total SWB area signals that storage infrastructure is contracting — through siltation, structural failure, or encroachment — rather than expanding to meet demand. Rapid drying between kharif and rabi seasons (low swb_kharif_area_ha persistence) indicates that tanks are not retaining sufficient water to support post-monsoon irrigation. Dashora et al. (2019) document that siltation of check dams in hard-rock monsoonal terrain reduces storage capacity and infiltration rates over time, with one 5-year-old check dam in Gujarat found completely filled with silt at an accumulation rate of 40 cm/year, directly impairing their function as irrigation water sources.

condition.qualitative_description: Number of surface water bodies is low relative to the micro-watershed area, indicating a structural gap in irrigation infrastructure coverage.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In AER-10 landscapes with plateau and slope terrain, surface water harvesting through tanks and ponds is the dominant irrigation modality. A low count of SWBs relative to the cultivated area of the micro-watershed indicates that the infrastructure network is insufficient to capture and store monsoon runoff for rabi and zaid use. This structural gap means that even in years of adequate rainfall, water is lost as runoff rather than being stored for dry-season irrigation. The absence of sufficient storage structures is a primary driver of seasonal water scarcity distinct from groundwater depletion.

condition.qualitative_description: Wells are deep (greater than 15 metres), implying high energy costs for lifting water that disadvantage marginal and small farmers from accessing groundwater as a supplementary irrigation source.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In semi-consolidated aquifer terrain of Bundelkhand and Malwa, groundwater can serve as a supplementary irrigation source when surface storage fails. However, deep wells require electric or diesel pumps with high operating costs, effectively excluding marginal farmers from groundwater-based irrigation. This cost barrier amplifies the impact of surface irrigation infrastructure failure: farmers who cannot afford to lift groundwater have no fallback when tanks dry up. Deep well depth in this context signals irrigation access inequality rather than aquifer depletion per se.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 10 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works completed under MGNREGA in the micro-watershed, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA SWC investment indicates that opportunities to build or rehabilitate tanks, check dams, and farm ponds have not been utilised. In Bundelkhand and Malwa, where surface water storage is the backbone of rabi irrigation, the absence of investment in new structures or desilting of existing ones directly perpetuates the infrastructure gap. Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrate that manual desilting of check dams increased recharge by 84% over the preceding year, with a benefit-cost ratio of 4.1 across four check dams in Rajasthan — evidence that MGNREGA-funded desilting is a high-return intervention. Low nrega_swc_count therefore signals both a current infrastructure deficit and an unrealised intervention opportunity.

condition.qualitative_description: Distance to nearest cooperative or credit society exceeds 10 km, indicating poor access to institutional credit for irrigation investment such as pump purchase, pipeline installation, or micro-irrigation systems.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Farmers in remote areas of Bundelkhand and Malwa who lack access to institutional credit cannot invest in individual irrigation solutions such as solar pumps, drip systems, or private storage structures. High distance to cooperatives therefore amplifies the impact of public infrastructure failure by removing the private investment fallback. This signal is particularly relevant for marginal farmers who are most vulnerable to seasonal water scarcity.

overall_reasoning_note: Irrigation infrastructure failure in AER-10 sub-humid plateau terrain should be confirmed by at least two primary signals: declining or low SWB area/count (sig_01, sig_02) combined with either low MGNREGA investment (sig_04) or deep costly wells (sig_03). A single signal may reflect data noise or a localised anomaly. The pathway is most strongly confirmed when surface water storage is demonstrably insufficient (low swb_count, rapid seasonal drying) AND there is evidence of low historical investment in infrastructure rehabilitation (low nrega_swc_count). Deep wells (sig_03) and poor cooperative access (sig_05) are amplifying signals that worsen outcomes but do not independently confirm the irrigation infrastructure pathway. Always distinguish this pathway from pure groundwater stress: here the primary failure is surface storage infrastructure, not aquifer depletion. Ask users whether existing tanks have been desilted recently and whether rabi irrigation is possible in good rainfall years — if yes, the infrastructure is present but degraded, confirming this pathway over a structural absence pathway.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought causing apparent irrigation infrastructure failure through reduced inflows to tanks and reservoirs, rather than structural inadequacy of the infrastructure itself
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare swb_total_area_ha and swb_kharif_area_ha trends against rainfall records. If SWB area decline co-occurs with documented drought years (low annual rainfall) but recovers in normal rainfall years, drought is the primary driver and infrastructure is adequate. If SWB area is low or declining even in normal or above-normal rainfall years, infrastructure inadequacy is confirmed. Ask users whether tanks filled adequately in recent good rainfall years — if tanks filled but dried quickly, siltation (reduced storage capacity) is the mechanism; if tanks never filled, catchment runoff capture is the problem.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater stress (declining aquifer levels) being misidentified as irrigation infrastructure failure when farmers shift from surface to groundwater irrigation and find wells insufficient
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If swb_count and swb_total_area_ha are adequate but farmers still report water scarcity, and annual_well_depth_m shows a multi-year increasing trend, the primary pathway is groundwater stress rather than surface infrastructure failure. In the irrigation infrastructure pathway, surface storage is the binding constraint; in the groundwater stress pathway, aquifer depletion is primary. Check whether farmers report that tanks are full but they still lack water — this would point to distribution or access failures rather than storage infrastructure gaps.

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Documents that siltation of check dams in monsoonal hard-rock terrain reduces storage capacity and infiltration rates, that manual desilting increased recharge by 84%, and that benefit-cost ratios of 4.1 make desilting economically attractive for securing irrigation water — directly supporting tank desilting via MGNREGA as a recommended solution and explaining the mechanism by which infrastructure degradation causes seasonal water scarcity.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 - Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Provides global context for increasing water scarcity under climate variability, relevant to understanding why seasonal irrigation infrastructure failures are becoming more frequent and severe in sub-humid regions like AER-10.

sources.title: Global Monthly Water Scarcity: Blue Water Footprints versus Blue Water Availability
sources.relevance_note: Documents that water scarcity is highly seasonal in river basins across India, with severe scarcity occurring during dry months — contextualising why rabi and zaid season irrigation failures are the primary manifestation of infrastructure inadequacy in sub-humid AER-10 settings.

recommended_solutions: Tank desilting and repair through MGNREGA
Micro-irrigation promotion (drip/sprinkler) to improve water use efficiency
Solar pump installation for groundwater lift irrigation
